entries.title: Outbound NTLM authentication blocked
entries.summary: An outbound NTLM authentication attempt was blocked by policy.
entries.details: Generated when an outbound NTLM authentication to a remote server is blocked by the 'Network Security: Restrict NTLM: Outgoing NTLM traffic to remote servers' policy. Key fields: TargetName (the server the client attempted to authenticate to), CallerProcessName, and ClientUserName. This event is useful for detecting forced NTLM authentication scenarios where malware or an adversary has redirected a victim's client toward an attacker-controlled SMB server to capture or relay NTLM credentials. The CallerProcessName field identifies the process that triggered the authentication — unexpected processes (browsers, Office applications) are high fidelity indicators of T1187 (Forced Authentication). Note: This channel requires NTLM auditing to be enabled via Group Policy.

prerequisites.description: The 'Network Security: Restrict NTLM: Outgoing NTLM traffic to remote servers' Group Policy must be configured to 'Deny all' or 'Audit all' for this event to be generated.

investigationPivots: CallerProcessName identifies what initiated the outbound NTLM attempt — non-system processes are suspicious
TargetName reveals the destination server; attacker-controlled IPs or external addresses are high fidelity
Correlate with web proxy or DNS logs if CallerProcessName is a browser — may indicate a UNC injection attack
commonFalsePositives: Applications using NTLM to authenticate to internal servers not listed in the NTLM exception list
Misconfigured services attempting NTLM auth to servers outside the allowed list

detectionRules.title: Forced NTLM Authentication Attempt from Unexpected Process
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-Security-NTLM"
| where EventID == 4001
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="CallerProcessName">' CallerProcessName '</Data>' *
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="TargetName">' TargetName '</Data>' *
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="ClientUserName">' ClientUserName '</Data>' *
| where CallerProcessName !has_any ("lsass.exe", "svchost.exe")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, CallerProcessName, TargetName, ClientUserName
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Requires NTLM outbound auditing enabled via Group Policy. A browser or Office app as CallerProcessName is a strong UNC injection / forced auth indicator.

entries.title: Incoming NTLM authentication blocked
entries.summary: An incoming NTLM authentication to this server was blocked by policy.
entries.details: Generated when an incoming NTLM authentication request to this server is blocked by the 'Network Security: Restrict NTLM: Incoming NTLM traffic' policy. Key fields: CallingMachineHostName (the client initiating the auth), ClientUserName, CallerProcessName, and Status. This event on servers provides visibility into which clients and accounts are still using NTLM to authenticate, which is critical for detecting relay attacks and identifying legacy authentication patterns. In relay attack scenarios, the ClientUserName and CallingMachineHostName may belong to a different victim machine than the attacker. Note: Requires enabling 'Network Security: Restrict NTLM: Audit Incoming NTLM Traffic' or 'Deny all accounts' via Group Policy.

prerequisites.description: The 'Network Security: Restrict NTLM: Incoming NTLM Traffic' Group Policy must be configured to 'Deny all accounts' or 'Audit all accounts' for this event to be generated.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay

investigationPivots: Identify all ClientUserName values attempting NTLM auth to this server — focus on privileged accounts
CallingMachineHostName reveals the originating client; cross-reference with known hosts
Repeated NTLM auth from a machine that should not be authenticating here is suspicious
commonFalsePositives: Legacy applications hardcoded to use NTLM authentication
Domain-joined clients authenticating to servers before Kerberos is available (startup timing)

entries.title: NTLM client authentication attempted (audit)
entries.summary: An outbound NTLM authentication from this client was recorded in audit mode.
entries.details: Generated when this system initiates an NTLM authentication as a client and the 'Network Security: Restrict NTLM: Audit Outgoing NTLM Traffic to Remote Servers' policy is set to 'Enable Auditing All'. Key fields: TargetName (the server being authenticated to), CallerProcessName, ClientUserName, and DomainName. This event provides full visibility into NTLM usage across the environment before enforcement is applied, making it essential for baselining NTLM traffic and identifying accounts or services that would break when NTLM restriction is enforced. Note: This event fires for all NTLM authentications, including legitimate ones, when auditing is enabled.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash

investigationPivots: Map all TargetName values to build a baseline of legitimate NTLM authentication targets
CallerProcessName authenticating to external or unexpected servers is a high-priority indicator
Filter for high-privilege accounts (Domain Admin, local Administrator) to prioritize pass-the-hash risk
commonFalsePositives: All legitimate NTLM authentication generates this event when auditing is enabled — volume is expected
Domain-joined hosts authenticating to workgroup or non-domain resources via NTLM

entries.title: NTLM server authentication received (audit)
entries.summary: This server received and processed an NTLM authentication request in audit mode.
entries.details: Generated when this system receives an incoming NTLM authentication as a server and the 'Network Security: Restrict NTLM: Audit Incoming NTLM Traffic' policy is enabled. Key fields: CallingMachineHostName, ClientUserName, CallerProcessName, and DomainName. This event provides the server-side view of all NTLM authentications, complementing EID 8001 on the client. In relay attack scenarios, the CallingMachineHostName may differ from the machine actually performing the relay — the real attacker machine appears only in network traffic, not this event. Correlate with EID 4624 in the Security log for logon success events.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay

investigationPivots: Identify any CallingMachineHostName values that do not match known internal hostnames
Correlate ClientUserName with EID 4624 on this host to confirm authentication succeeded
High-privilege account authentication via NTLM (rather than Kerberos) is anomalous on modern domain-joined systems
commonFalsePositives: Services and scheduled tasks using NTLM to authenticate to this server
Non-domain clients (workgroup machines) that can only use NTLM

entries.title: NTLM passthrough authentication to domain controller (audit)
entries.summary: An NTLM authentication was passed through to a domain controller for validation.
entries.details: Generated on a domain controller when it receives a passthrough NTLM authentication request from a member server or workstation. Key fields: CallingMachineHostName (the host forwarding the request), ClientUserName, and DomainName. On domain controllers this event provides insight into which member servers are receiving NTLM authentication and forwarding it for validation, helping to identify unexpected NTLM flows and legacy authentication paths. Overpass-the-hash (T1550.002) attacks that use a stolen NTLM hash to forge a Kerberos ticket do not generate this event — but traditional pass-the-hash via SMB will if auditing is enabled.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash

investigationPivots: Correlate CallingMachineHostName with the expected authentication path to identify abnormal NTLM flows
Combine with Security log EID 4776 (credential validation) on the DC to map the full authentication chain
NTLM passthrough for privileged accounts (Domain Admin) is high priority regardless of success or failure
commonFalsePositives: Legacy applications on member servers authenticating users via NTLM passthrough to the DC
Workstations accessing resources on servers that do not have Kerberos configured

entries.title: Incoming NTLM authentication to server blocked (audit)
entries.summary: An incoming NTLM authentication request to this server was recorded as blocked or audited.
entries.details: Generated when an incoming NTLM authentication to this server is blocked or audited under the 'Network Security: Restrict NTLM: Incoming NTLM Traffic' policy set to 'Deny all domain accounts' or 'Audit all'. Key fields: CallingMachineHostName, ClientUserName, CallerProcessName, Status, and DomainName. This event is the enforcement-mode complement to EID 8002. When an NTLM relay attack succeeds, the relayed authentication may appear here as a successful authentication from an unexpected machine. A ClientUserName matching a privileged account arriving from an unexpected CallingMachineHostName is a high-fidelity indicator of credential relay.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay
Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash

investigationPivots: Privileged account (ClientUserName) arriving from an unexpected CallingMachineHostName is a relay attack indicator
Correlate with LLMNR/NBT-NS events on the network to identify the poisoning phase of a relay chain
Cross-reference CallingMachineHostName with that machine's own EID 8001 events to verify it initiated the auth
commonFalsePositives: Domain accounts legitimately authenticating via NTLM before the exception list is fully configured
Mixed-environment scenarios with non-domain members needing NTLM access

detectionRules.title: Privileged Account NTLM Auth from Unexpected Source
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-Security-NTLM"
| where EventID == 8004
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="ClientUserName">' ClientUserName '</Data>' *
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="CallingMachineHostName">' CallingMachineHostName '</Data>' *
| where ClientUserName has_any ("admin", "Administrator")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, ClientUserName, CallingMachineHostName
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Correlate CallingMachineHostName against the victim host's EID 8001 logs to verify the machine actually initiated the auth — in relay attacks the true origin differs.
